refinement_sections.content: The primary objective is to assess whether the average sales of the newly launched product exceed a predetermined benchmark. This involves formulating a clear null hypothesis (H0) and an alternative hypothesis (H1) based on the benchmark value. For instance, if the benchmark average sales are $X, then H0: μ ≤ X and H1: μ > X, where μ represents the true average sales of the product.

Data should be collected from the sales team's records over the past three months, ensuring that the dataset is representative and free from biases. This includes total sales figures, the number of units sold, and any relevant demographic information about the customers that could influence sales.

To conduct the hypothesis test, I will utilize a one-sample t-test if the sample size is small (n < 30) and the population standard deviation is unknown, or a z-test if the sample size is large (n ≥ 30). The significance level (α) will be set at 0.05. It is crucial to check for normality in the sales data, as this will affect the choice of test. If the data is not normally distributed, a non-parametric test such as the Wilcoxon signed-rank test may be appropriate.

Key assumptions include: (1) the sales data is independent and identically distributed, (2) the sample size is sufficiently large for the Central Limit Theorem to apply if using a z-test, and (3) the sales figures are normally distributed if using a t-test. Any violations of these assumptions should be addressed, as they can lead to incorrect conclusions.

After conducting the hypothesis test, I will calculate the p-value and compare it to the significance level. If the p-value is less than α, we will reject the null hypothesis, indicating that the average sales exceed the benchmark. The confidence interval for the mean sales will also be computed to provide a range of plausible values for the true average sales.

The final report will include a detailed methodology section, results of the hypothesis test, interpretation of findings, and recommendations based on the analysis. It will also discuss the implications of the results for future marketing strategies and product adjustments, if necessary. Any limitations of the study and suggestions for further research will also be included.
